entry.title: Re: Requirement Date in Subcontracting Component SAP S4 H...
entry.content:
Hello @AngelT 

Thank you for your question.

As you probably know, in subcontracting, the component Requirement Date is scheduled backward from your PO date of delivery schedule.

Based on your description and attached screenshots (very helpful), even if the finished material delivery date comes from your info record, the system calculates the subcontracting component date separately because it uses the scheduling logic to ascertain your component availability. That means the system subtracts in practice time elements from the delivery date you scheduled. For example, these elements could be:

  1. Component side procurement or your lead time settings
  2. Your plant level purchasing processing time and related scheduling parameters you defined.
  3. It also takes the calendar logic into consideration, e.g., working days vs. Calendar days.

That explains why you can get “about 4 days earlier” in one case and “about 7 days” after you make manual date changes. KBA 2176766 deals with details on requirement dates calculation. You can also find the standard backward calculation pattern by consulting KBA 3023022.

So, in public cloud, the standard configuration and master data checks allows you to analyse the date and it allows you to adjust fields that influence your scheduling. However, it does permit you to freely rewrite core subcontracting date algorithms or use classic on-premise exits to override all standard date logic.

Here is what i suggest you do:

  1. In your system compare your PO schedule line delivery date vs your component requirement date

  1. Check your SSCUI 102156 plant purchasing processing time
  2. Check your component material MRP and procurement relevant data
  3. Examine your source related lead time inputs
  4. Then finally re-test those with date shifts to validate the behaviour of workday or calendar day

See the following references for more details:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/6e52f71dfb5a481b810ce0697708baf2.html 

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/023f62758f7a44cf904eaef9035e0079.html 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Prevent negative balance in Investment WBS – SAP S/4H...
entry.content:
Good day @AlvaroFernandezMorales 

Thank you for your question.

As you mentioned, event-based settlement supports reversal posting triggering automatic settlement reversal logic. However, settlement is periodic in non event-based settlement. So reversing GR after settlement can leave the WBS temporarily negative until you carry out settlement steps. So, event-based settlement is the approach to avoid periodic settlement timing gaps. See https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/ee9ee0ca4c3942068ea584d2f929b5b1/70df5e3ad2cc41d9aeec6b3b9766d17e.html for more details.

Here are a few areas to consider to prevent negative WBS balances:

  1. If your business allows it, the cleanest way to prevent negative WBS balances is to use event-based settlement profile for your investment projects.
  2. If you must use non event based, then use an operational rule like, reverse GR, immediately run settlement again for your affected WBS and period & recheck sender balance before period close. Note that doing that does not remove the design characteristics but it prevents residual negative balance from remaining open.
  3. You may set up period end checks for WBS with negative actual balance, WBS that was settled in period with subsequent reversals and exception lists that must be re settled before you signoff the close. The public cloud offers this through reporting and close governance and not with custom ABAP exits.
  4. If your projects are not event based, I suggest you avoid running your final settlement before you are sure GR and reversals are complete for the period or run an additional post reversal settlement cycle as mandatory step in close calendar.

To summarise:

  • In a non event-based mode it is expected for you to have negative WBS after GR reversal unless you re settle.
  • The best way to prevent negative WBS after GR reversal is to use event based settlement or carry out a strict “reversal then immediately re settlement” close control.

See following reference for more details:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c56f622a2edf491b9f1b596b55587009/a9a5b9fd76ac481abf6893dba930ab15.html?locale=en-US

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

Could you run READ_TEXT via SE37, with ID = ZEIN (the text ID you used), LANGUAGE = EN, NAME = sales order number with leading zeroes (total 10 digits), OBJECT = VBBK, to make sure that the format is the one you passed.

entry.title: Re: MR21 and MDG
entry.content:
Hi @panagiotism ,
My friend MR21-based price changes do not flow through SAP MDG, and using LSMW on MR21 will not update prices in an MDG-governed environment. This is expected behavior, not a mistake in your setup.

MR21 cannot be used through SAP MDG. MR21 updates valuation data (MBEW) directly in the ERP system, and MDG does not govern or replicate these price changes. That’s why your LSMW recording works in ECC/S4 but the price does not appear updated in MDG. MDG simply doesn’t manage MR21-type price updates.

If you need MDG to handle prices, it requires a custom MDG data model extension, because SAP does not provide this out of the box.

entry.title: Re: FX Diff/Gain Not Posting in Posting area 2 (Auto Clea...
entry.content:
Hi @Aliice ,
I would like to give you clear explanation, why FX gain/loss is not posting automatically in Posting Area 2 during automatic bank statement clearing — and why it does post when you manually reprocess Posting Area 2 — along with the configuration points you should check. Your issue is a known behavior in SAP S/4HANA:
FX differences are only posted automatically if the clearing logic in Posting Area 2 is triggered with full information about the original open item.

From the SAP Community description of your exact scenario, the system does post FX differences correctly when Posting Area 2 is manually reprocessed, but not during automatic clearing.

Check Posting Area 2 Variant (OT83), Make sure the variant used for Posting Area 2 include.
Posting rule for FX differences
The posting rule must include:

  • Exchange rate gain account
  • Exchange rate loss account
  • Clearing account logic

If these accounts are missing, SAP cannot post FX differences automatically.

entry.title: Re: SAP BTP - Integration Suite - ( DCRP + PDCP ) : Implementation & Performance Guide (Part III
entry.content:
@vinaymittal  

You’re right, I missed that part in my answer and didn’t complement it properly.

If 10 different sender systems call the same domain endpoint (e.g. api.company.com/sales/*), how is access control enforced to prevent cross‑sender access?

1. Why I do not recommend a raw /sales/* model
In my first blog (which has now been removed), I explicitly discouraged a universal wildcard such as /sales/*. I tested exactly this approach: a single proxy for all processes, routing based only on proxy.pathsuffix = "/*".

The result was clear: it opens the door to serious security and governance risks, because “anything after /sales” is potentially accepted. To mitigate this, I initially built a JavaScript Security Shield that would immediately block any path not present in a predefined table, returning HTTP 403. That experiment is precisely what pushed me toward a stricter, metadata‑driven model.

JavaScript Threat Protection (Security Shield)

  • Purpose: Protect a /** catch‑all route from malicious requests.
  • Author: Ricardo Viana.
  • Runs: Before JS routing, in ProxyEndpoint PreFlow.

Why it is required in a catch‑all scenario:

  • Catch‑all route (/**) means every request reaches the routing engine.
  • Without the shield: path traversal, injection attempts, and domain hijacking all reach KVM/JS.
  • With the shield: they are blocked at PreFlow with HTTP 403, saving CPU, latency and reducing blast radius.

Example whitelist configuration - it's accepts /sales/** but if it's out of the list below, HTTP 403

// ============================================
// SECTION 2: DOMAIN WHITELIST VALIDATION
// ============================================
var allowedDomains = [
“sales”, “orders”, “customers”, “invoices”, “prices”, “quotations”,
“finance”, “accounts”, “journals”, “payments”, “reconciliations”,
“reports”, “closings”, “taxes”, “budgets”,
“logistics”, “shipments”, “inventory”, “deliveries”, “warehouses”,
“tracking”, “carriers”, “returns”, “pickpack”,
“procurement”, “requisitions”, “suppliers”, “contracts”,
“buyers”, “approvals”, “receipts”, “catalogs”
];
 

Flow execution:

  • ProxyEndpoint PreFlow

    • JS-Security-Shield → validates whitelist, blocks attacks.
    • RF-SecurityThreat → returns 403 if threat detected.
    • Quota + Spike Arrest → rate limiting.

  • TargetEndpoint PreFlow
    4. KVM lookup + routing → only if security passed.

This “shielded catch‑all” was my first step. For production‑scale performance, I evolved it to the current model, where the engine reads cpi.basepath at runtime and relies on strict KVM entries instead of a broad /** route.

2. How GDCR actually generates and secures endpoints
Instead of relying on /sales/*, GDCR translates domain + process + vendor into explicit metadata entries.

Example domain façade:

  • https://<apim>/sales/customers/sync/salesforceemea
  • https://<apim>/sales/customers/sync/salesforceus

Both calls hit the same Sales domain proxy, but the Maverick Engine reads the path, normalizes the action, and builds a deterministic KVM key (entity + action + vendor). That key maps to concrete CPI endpoints:

Resulting backend URLs in CPI:

  • https://<cpi>/dcrp/http/customers/s/id02emea
  • https://<cpi>/dcrp/http/customers/s/id02us

The iFlow ID is logically the same (id02), but the regional suffix (emea vs us) is encoded in the KVM and in the final URL, so each vendor/region combination is a separate, explicit route.

So, going back to your generic example api.company.com/sales/*, in GDCR it materializes as concrete paths like:

  • https://<apim>/sales/customers/sync/salesforceemea
  • https://<apim>/sales/customers/sync/salesforceus

And those, in turn, resolve to:

  • https://<cpi>/dcrp/http/customers/s/id02emea
  • https://<cpi>/dcrp/http/customers/s/id02us

Access control is enforced at two levels:

  • Product / scope level: API products and scopes decide which paths each sender is allowed to call.
  • Metadata level: the Maverick Engine will only route to endpoints that have an exact KVM match for that domain + action + vendor combination. Anything outside that dictionary fails fast.

3. Historical perspective on patterns
What I am doing with GDCR / DCRP / PDCP follows the same trajectory as other global patterns:

  • GoF – Design Patterns – ~5–8 years to global adoption.​
  • Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture (P of EAA) – ~5–10 years.​
  • Enterprise Integration Patterns (EIP) – ~5–10 years.
  • Domain‑Driven Design (DDD) – ~10–15 years.
  • Clean / Hexagonal / Onion Architecture – ~8–12 years.
  • EIP implemented in products (ESB, iPaaS, brokers) – ~5–10 years.
  • GDCR ? The time will show.

GDCR was released only 15 days ago as a hybrid, multi‑vendor, globally scalable pattern, fully abstracted from the technical layer and focused on the core business semantics. I do not expect the market to “understand” it overnight. The realistic horizon is 5–15 years, just like the patterns above.

In the end, I’m at peace with that. I built, documented, and validated the framework alone – 106,190+ messages across 4 scenarios with 0 routing failures – because I believe metadata‑driven, domain‑centric routing is not a bottleneck; it’s simply the next step.

At this point, what I would really like to do is plug this into a real production environment and fire something like:

 
start "" newman run gdcr-maverick-v15-2-phantom.json --iteration-count 117 --reporters cli,json --reporter-json-export results-run-1.json --color on
start "" newman run gdcr-maverick-v15-2-phantom.json --iteration-count 117 --reporters cli,json --reporter-json-export results-run-2.json --color on
start "" newman run gdcr-maverick-v15-2-phantom.json --iteration-count 117 --reporters cli,json --reporter-json-export results-run-3.json --color on
start "" newman run gdcr-maverick-v15-2-phantom.json --iteration-count 117 --reporters cli,json --reporter-json-export results-run-4.json --color on
start "" newman run gdcr-maverick-v15-2-phantom.json --iteration-count 117 --reporters cli,json --reporter-json-export results-run-5.json --color on

If the machine survives that, then this solution is ready for truly galactic‑scale workloads.

On a personal note, I’m genuinely happy with where this journey brought me. I don’t really care if this framework “explodes” in terms of popularity or not. What matters to me is that I had the discipline to move beyond the narrow “SAP CPI developer” label and the usual box‑drawing, mapping‑only mindset, and grow into an enterprise integration architect focused on the whole SAP BTP Integration Suite ecosystem as DCRP + PDCP and Global Multi-vendor API e Orquestraction with GDCR, I can apply this solution everywhere, mostly by studying alone over the last four months. This is beautiful to me as a professional and, even more, for my family; the rest is fine — we move forward.

I’m also a writer at heart. I love to write, and I will keep writing and sharing wherever I can, because sharing knowledge helps everyone grow and that’s what really matters to me. Of course, the final core logic of this solution will not be published in full detail — deep, production‑grade knowledge and IP must be treated as something you invest in and pay for.

This will also be my final reply on this topic in this forum, so that the pattern, its guarantees, and its limits remain crystal‑clear and self‑contained in a single thread.

Now I need to get back to work and finish the GitHub and documents properly.

The Commander | Viana

Enterprise Integration Architect - Creator of GDCR

entry.title: SAP Identity Provisioning Service - All Available Connectors for User sync from Source to Target
entry.content:
Why this matters. In a top‑down user provisioning model, you drive identities from one (or a few) authoritative sources into many downstream business applications. SAP Identity Provisioning (IPS) acts as the orchestration layer: it reads users/groups/roles from sources, optionally transforms them, and writes them into targets.

Picking the right connector—and knowing what each one supports—lets you design flows that are robust, secure, and easy to maintain.
 

How to read the catalog

Each connector below lists:

  • Display Name & Type – How it appears in IPS and the internal connector key you reference in jobs.
  • Destination Type – The protocol or API family IPS uses (HTTP, LDAP, RFC, SSH, etc.).
  • Supported Entities – What IPS can read from that system in a top‑down context (e.g., user, group, role, or event).
  • Visibility / Enabled – Whether the connector is visible in the UI and currently enabled.
  • Destination Required – Whether the connector expects a dedicated “Destination” object (e.g., RFC for ABAP).

Tip: For enterprise-scale top‑down setups, a common pattern is Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) or Identity Authentication (IAS) as the authoritative source, fanning out to S/4HANA, SuccessFactors, Ariba, Fieldglass, SAP BTP services, and any non‑SAP apps via SCIM or LDAP. Use IPS transformations (mappings, filters) to normalize attributes across targets.

SAP IPS Connector - Supported Systems

#Connector TypeDisplay NameDestination TypeSupported EntitiesVisibleRequiredEnabled Destination

entry.title: How to Lookup Employee Info in SuccessFactors Business Rule?
entry.content:
Background

The Lookup() function is one of the most frequently used features in both Excel and SAP SuccessFactors business rules.
In Employee Central (EC), common scenarios include:

  • Looking up entitled annual leave based on seniority maintained in a custom MDF object
  • Performing value replacement or mapping using a custom MDF configuration table
    In most standard use cases, Lookup works smoothly — as long as the target object is MDF-based.

Challenge

However, limitations appear when attempting cross-module or cross-entity lookups.
Typical challenges include:

  1. HRIS elements (e.g., EmpJob, User) are not supported as Lookup targets
    The Lookup function only supports MDF objects. Direct lookup on HRIS tables such as empJob or user is not allowed.
  2. Effective-dated logic constraints
    SuccessFactors Business rules only return the latest record as of today if using MDF or other as start navigation entity, making it difficult to retrieve employee information for a specific historical period.
    These restrictions become more visible in cross-module scenarios.

Business Requirements

Scenario 1 – Recruiting

In Recruiting, you may need to retrieve the contact information of a specific HR personnel who is not configured as a standard operator in the job requisition.
As we know:

  • The number of standard operators is limited
  • You cannot navigate to arbitrary employee entities in Recruiting rules execpt the pre-defined operators (e.g., Recruiter, Hiring Manager)
  • Auto-populating HR contact information becomes difficult

Scenario 2 – Identifying Manager Role

A customer needs to determine whether an employee is a manager.
However:

  • There is no standard boolean field in Employment Information that directly identifies “Manager”
  • The information may exist in Position or Job Information
  • It is not easily accessible via Lookup in business rules

Technical Solution

Considering the following facts:

  1. Lookup only supports MDF objects, not HRIS elements
  2. Integration Center is a built-in lightweight middleware tool
  3. SuccessFactors can act as both Source and Target system
    A practical workaround is:

Replicate key HRIS data (e.g., Job Information) into a custom MDF object using a scheduled Integration Center job.
This makes the data accessible through Lookup in business rules.

Step 1 – Create a Custom MDF Object

Design an MDF object to mirror required EC fields.

Key Design Principles

  • externalCode must be String type
  • Use an onSave rule to auto-populate:
    userId + "_" + effectiveStartDate
  • This ensures uniqueness for effective-dated records

Recommended Fields

You could add the custom fields based on business requirement.

Field IDType Source Mapping (empJob)
externalCodeStringuserId + effectiveDate
effectiveStartDateDatestartDate
cust_endDateDateendDate
cust_managerIdStringmanagerId

Step 2 – Create an Integration Center Job

Integration Design

  • Source System: SuccessFactors
  • Target System: SuccessFactors
  • A single scheduled job is sufficient.

Key Configuration Points

  • Starting Entity: empJob
    This covers global assignment and multiple employment scenarios via userId.
  • Advanced Filter: effectiveLatestChange eq true
    Ensures only the latest change on the same effective date is selected.
  • Time Filter:
    Set "From Date" = 1900-01-01
    This ensures historical records are included.
  • Schedule:
    Daily job (recommended during off-peak hours)

Result

This approach provides a lightweight, stable, and scalable workaround within standard SuccessFactors capabilities to effectively converts non-supported HRIS data into rule-friendly MDF data, after replication:

  • The custom MDF object becomes available in Business Rules
  • Lookup can retrieve cross-module employee information
  • Effective-dated records are preserved
  • Complex navigation limitations are avoided

entry.title: ServiceNow Incident to CALM Defect - Incident Management
entry.content:
Dear colleagues,

Our customer is currently using SNOW as their ITSM tool and CALM as their project management tool. We are also preparing for the upcoming go-live of the S/4HANA solution.

Since SNOW is comparatively more complex to use and manage, we would like to explore whether it is possible during Hypercare phase to automatically create a defect in CALM whenever an incident is created in SNOW. Important fields would be description of the issue, priority and scope/responsible etc. 

Could you please advise if such an integration or automation is possible? I would be also happy if i can show customer in written format such an extension/integration possible so do we have any one-pager, presentation etc? 

Thank you !

#CloudALM

entry.title: Re: Any standard utility in ABAP to retrieve a version of...
entry.content:
I guess you're interested only by the last version in PROD, and you want to copy it from PROD to DEV. For the last version and for any kind of object, possibly SVRS_GET_VERSION_REMOTE. NB: if the question had been about retrieving any version remotely, I don't know.

For copying code of the active version, I recommend using a transport of copies, but you must copy the whole object (R3TR). For instance, if you find out that only one method of a class is different, you'll have to transport the whole class. Your DEV system must be configured with VERS_AT_IMP = ALWAYS to keep the version history. In case your object source system is not DEV (the system ID of your DEV system), I recommend changing it to DEV.

The function modules for working with versions and transport requests are not released by SAP so you may use them at your own risk.

What you could do:

  1. I think it's best to create an RFC-enabled Z function module to be installed on your PROD system, which calls:
    1. TRINT_INSERT_COMM to create a ToC,
    2. TRINT_RELEASE_REQUEST to release the ToC. There's BAPI_CTREQUEST_RELEASE too but it has limited features.
    3. Read the created files in the cofiles and data directories (be careful the release may run asynchronously, so you may need to wait a little bit), return the data from these files.
  2. In the DEV system:
    1. Create these files in the cofiles and data directories of your DEV system (if the transport domain of DEV and PRD are different),
    2. Add the transport request to the import queue via TMS_MGR_GREP_TRANSPORT_QUEUE and TMS_MGR_FORWARD_TR_REQUEST
    3. Import via TMS_MGR_IMPORT_TR_REQUEST.
    4. NB: you may get the transport domain via TMS_CFG_GET_DOMAIN_NAME.

NB: I never saw a customer asking for such a risky program (automating things between PROD and DEV systems -- the PROD system should never be connected to a non-production system for security reason --, overwriting objects without control) and I would never suggest doing this, so it's really at your own risk.

Make sure that the client understands the risks.

entry.title: SAP SuccessFactors: Build your Adoption Program​ to Maximize Results (English; Europe & Americas)
entry.content:
PLEASE NOTE: Selecting the ‘Will you be attending?’ option will NOT register you for the event.

To register for this event, please click here: Register Here

SAP SuccessFactors: Build your Adoption Program​ to Maximize Results (English; Europe & Americas)

This session will help you understand the relevant components of an adequate adoption program to maximize your cloud investment and ensure sustained business value. We will walk you through the essentials to orchestrate Change Management, Governance, Sustainment, and Value Management and how they work together to drive ongoing adoption, solution optimization and user experience.​ Participants will explore best practices and take away actionable templates to build an initial transformation roadmap, define clear roles and responsibilities, and identify the right executive sponsors and stakeholder groups.​ This webcast is a foundational session that will lead into optional follow‑on topic specific webcasts on your preferred topics.. At the end, you will leave with a concrete structure and toolkit to start or refine your own SuccessFactors adoption program.

Please contact SAP_SuccessFactors_Onboarding@sap.com for any questions on this event.

Here is a link to our Global schedule

entry.title: Re: how to give new line in segment E1BPSDTEXT of idoc SALESORDER_CREATEFROMDAT2
entry.content: I couldn't find any SAP note so I advise contacting SAP Support. A workaround is to call the function modules INIT_TEXT, SAVE_TEXT and COMMIT_TEXT directly (only SAVE_TEXT is mandatory, the two others depend on the parameters you passed to SAVE_TEXT), followed by a COMMIT WORK.

entry.title: Re: SAP to Microsoft Excel Integration for trial balance
entry.content:
Dear @AmitKalaskar,

For Public Cloud the standard API you want for a Trial Balance extract is:

  • Trial Balance Read (OData V2), Technical name C_TrialBalance_CDS
    It returns starting/debit/credit/ending balances for G/L accounts
  • It is delivered under the communication scenario Finance Accounting Analytics Integration (SAP_COM_0303)

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/b978f98fc5884ff2aeb10c8fdeb8a43b/61ab22d2f6da49a394004e459947e994.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/6b39bd1d0e5e4099a5b65d835c29c696/5792333ddf3c47eaad4314b071dfd684.html

Step 1.

A. Create a Communication User

1. App Communication Users ->New

2. Create user + password

B. Create a Communication System

1. App Communication Systems ->New

2. Maintain a system name/id

3. In Users for Inbound Communication assign the Communication User you created

C. Create a Communication Arrangement

1. App Communication Arrangements ->New

2. Choose communication scenario SAP_COM_0303

 

3. Select your Communication System

4. Look for the inbound service Trial Balance Read / C_TrialBalance_CDS

5. Copy the service URL shown in the inbound services to use this host for Excel

Step 2.

This API is commonly consumed via the function import + /Results with mandatory PostingDate From/To parameters plus filters and a $select otherwise it can time out

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3588269

Why this matters:

  • Provide a filter at least Ledger + CompanyCode
  • Use $select / aggregation level to avoid timeouts
  • Too many selected fields or too wide ranges can trigger backend DB errors

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3689885

Step 3.

Follow the steps from the blog mentioned in your question:

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/s-4hana-cloud-integration-microsoft-excel/bc-p/13387926

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Deep Dive into SAP Datasphere Object Store of BDC: Benefits, Architecture and implementation
entry.content:
In this document, I will go through the architecture and benefits of HANA DataLake Files service in the BDC Datasphere application. In the SAP Business business Data cloud there are 2 instances of HDLF object stores,

  1. Foundation services HDLF object store - managed by SAP
  2. Datasphere  HDLF object store - managed by Customers

Object store is a file storage from Hyperscalers like Amazon s3 to store large amounts of data files(csv, parquet and delta tables)

FOS HDLF object store is used for SAP standard data products, when an intelligent application is activated in BDC Cockpit, required standard data products are installed and data is loaded to FOS HDLF object store. these Data products are available as Delta tables in the FOS HDLF, can be shared with Datasphere HANA Space or Databricks for data analytics and data science models. FOS HDLF is managed by SAP, Customers don't have access to it. 

Datasphere HDLF object store is used for storing large data files from SAP and non SAP systems. these files are available as Delta tables. Customers can configure the ETL to replicate and transform data into Datasphere HDLF space. Also, custom data products can be generated in the Datasphere HDLF from BW PCE in BDC. 

In this article, we focus on the Datasphere HDLF object store.

HDLF object store was made available in Standalone Datasphere(BTP) as part of data fabric solution from SAP, later it is introduced as part of BDC.

Benefits:

  • HDLF object store is cost effective solution,  1TB is 0.1 CU per hour.
  • Spark compute for delta table creation, scalable runtime for transformation flows in the HDLF space.
  • "SQL on files" feature, read data from HDLF space into HANA cloud models for analytics.

You can check the price quote for the object store and compute in the BDC price estimator https://bdc-pricing-estimator-sac-sacus10.cfapps.us10.hana.ondemand.com/

Architecture:

  • Embedded HDLF object store in BDC Datasphere is optional.
  • It requires additional capacity units, BDC pricing estimator can provide the number.
  • Data is replicated from Source systems and stored as Delta tables using Replication flows.
  • Delta tables are parquet files with transaction logs and maintained in the HDLF object store.
  • SQL on Files engine can read data from Delta tables of HDLF object store in HANA cloud without loading them to HANA Database. It is a Native HANA feature and explained in detail in this SAP blog SQL on Files 
  • Apache SPARK compute provides scalable runtime for the transformation flows to process delta table(Parquet files) from Bronze to Silver to Gold layer Delta tables(Medallion architecture).

Configuration of Datasphere object store

The minimum capacity units required to activate Object Store in BDC is 4150.05 CU (128 GB of SAP HANA memory). You can Simulate price the BDC estimator, steps are provided in the SAP blog Objectstore CUs

After that you can configure the Tenant in Datasphere --> System --> Configuration --> Tenant configuration and add the Object store units. You can find the detail steps in the SAP blog Configuring the Tenant

after that, when we create a new space in Datasphere, option to choose HANA cloud or HDLF space will be enabled.

HANA Space Vs HDLF Space in Datasphere:

After configuring HDLF object store, we have 2 spaces in Datasphere.

  • HANA space
  • HANA DataLake file space. 

HANA cloud space is the traditional space available in the Standalone Datasphere tenant(BTP), it requires HANA memory and HANA compute engine to store and process the data. On the other side, HDLF space is available in the BDC Datasphere and uses SPARK compute and object storage.

we must choose the space that we intend to work and build the objects accordingly. monitoring of jobs are specific to the space. Overall the look and feel experience for object development and monitoring jobs is same in both spaces.

In the HDLF space, replication flows and transformation flows are used to replicate and transform the large data files. Modeling objects like Graphical or SQL views and analytic models can't be developed in this space.

We can share the HDLF local delta tables(files) in HANA space and use them in transformation flows and Vice versa. that is Share the HANA space Local tables in a HDLF space and use them transformation flows.

End to end process flows Replication flow + Transformation flows

Replication flows are used to integrate/replicate data from Source systems into Inbound buffer first, then a merge task needs to be executed to write data to Delta table file. Transformation flows are used to transform the data to silver and gold layers of Delta table files in HDLF object store. These Delta tables can be shared in HANA space of Datasphere in the view builder.

Importance of merge task:

Since the Local tables (files) contains large data volumes, merge task updates the data by allocating required amount of SPARK compute resources. You can enable the merge task directly in the Replication flow configuration or manually run it from the monitor. You can schedule it as a task in the Task chains. Now you can cancel a running merge task in the monitor tab.  Merge task is to be required for BW Data product generator delta table files also.

Note: In case of merge task is not run, then data is not available for reporting.

Python script can be written in the transformation flows and run with SPARK compute to process large data files.

Data product generator for Object store in Datasphere:

Data products are created as Local Delta tables(files) in the object store of Datasphere from BW InfoProviders. For the Subscriptions that are created in BW PCE, even delta data can be pushed from BW ADSO into Object store. these delta local tables can be shared in other applications.

Source from SAP

More detail explanation on the BW data products can be found this blog Data Product Generator

Limitations of Object store in Datasphere:

  • Replication Flows currently do not support local table (files) as source. So only Inbound.
  • Only 5 HDLF spaces can be created in Datasphere tenant.
  • Objects without Primary key cannot be loaded into embedded object store
  • Support of local type “initial load” only in Replication Flows is not supported.
  • Some datatypes don't support in Replication flows of HDLF space.
  • You will need to check the connection documentation to make sure if it is supported for Data lake files or not.

Summary: SAP Datasphere object store is a cost effective solution to store large data files as delta tables and transform the data with SPARK runtime and share them as data products in SAP Databricks.

entry.title: Understanding SAP HANA High Availability - Technical Scope, Mechanisms, and Design Boundaries
entry.content:
Introduction
SAP HANA provides a set of explicitly documented mechanisms to support high availability (HA) in productive landscapes. These mechanisms are designed to minimize unplanned downtime, enable fast continuation of operations, and preserve transactional consistency when specific failure types occur.

High availability in SAP HANA is not a single feature. It is an architecture composed of multiple building blocks, each with a clearly documented technical objective, scope, and boundary. These building blocks are described across the SAP HANA Administration Guide and related SAP Help Portal documentation.

This article consolidates SAP-documented SAP HANA high-availability mechanisms into a single technical view.
Its purpose is to make scope, interaction, and design boundaries explicit, enabling technically sound architecture design, realistic availability expectations, and precise discussions.

All statements are derived from official SAP documentation.

1. High Availability in SAP HANA - Technical Definition
From a technical perspective, high availability in SAP HANA focuses on:

  • minimizing unplanned downtime
  • enabling fast system continuation after certain failure types
  • preserving transactional consistency during failover or takeover

High availability is explicitly distinct from:

  • backup and recovery, which restore data to a previous state
  • disaster recovery, which addresses site-level or regional failures

This separation of objectives is consistently reflected in SAP HANA documentation and tooling.

Key boundary
High availability mechanisms maintain continuity of operation, not historical restore points.

2. SAP HANA High Availability Building Blocks
SAP HANA high availability is achieved by combining several documented mechanisms:

  • Host Auto-Failover
  • SAP HANA System Replication
  • Scale-Up and Scale-Out topologies
  • SAP-supported cluster frameworks on certified operating systems (for example, Pacemaker)

Each mechanism addresses specific failure classes and operates within a defined technical scope. No single mechanism covers all failure scenarios.

3. Host Auto-Failover - Technical Scope and Boundaries
3.1 Purpose and Mechanism
Host Auto-Failover is designed to handle host-level failures within a SAP HANA landscape.
In supported configurations:

  • a standby host is configured
  • SAP HANA detects a host failure
  • database services are automatically reassigned to the standby host

This reassignment is performed by SAP HANA itself based on internal monitoring and configuration.

SAP Help Portal reference
SAP HANA Host Auto-Failover

3.2 Technical Objective
Host Auto-Failover focuses on:

  • host failure detection
  • service reassignment
  • automatic continuation of operations

It does not:

  • provide historical restore points
  • protect against logical data errors
  • replace backup or recovery mechanisms

Key boundary
Host Auto-Failover preserves service execution, not data history.

4. SAP HANA System Replication in the HA Context
4.1 Technical Role
SAP HANA System Replication (SR) is a core building block for high availability and disaster tolerance scenarios.
In the HA context, SR:

  • continuously transfers data changes from a primary system to a secondary system
  • enables fast takeover in case of primary system failure
  • preserves transactional consistency at takeover time

SAP Help Portal reference
SAP HANA System Replication - Overview

4.2 Replication Modes and Availability Behavior
SAP HANA System Replication supports different replication modes, such as synchronous and asynchronous replication.
From a high-availability perspective:

  • the replication mode defines when changes are acknowledged
  • the secondary system reflects the current state of the primary system

Logical changes, deletions, or corruptions on the primary system are replicated as part of normal operation.
This behavior is inherent to replication and aligns with the documented objective of SR as an availability mechanism.

SAP Help Portal reference
SAP HANA System Replication - Replication Modes

Key boundary
System replication maintains current state, not historical restore points.

5. Cluster Management and Pacemaker – Technical Role
5.1 Purpose of Cluster Frameworks
In supported Linux-based SAP HANA environments, cluster frameworks such as Pacemaker are used to orchestrate:

  • system and service monitoring
  • failover and takeover actions
  • role coordination between primary and secondary systems

Pacemaker does not replace SAP HANA availability mechanisms.
It coordinates and automates actions around them.

SAP HANA System Replication with Cluster Management (Pacemaker)
(Implementation is covered by OS-vendor specific best-practice guides, not by a single SAP HANA Platform help page.)

5.2 Design Boundary
From a technical perspective:

  • Pacemaker does not replicate data
  • Pacemaker does not provide restore points
  • Pacemaker does not replace SAP HANA logic

It acts as an orchestration layer for documented HA mechanisms.

6. Failure Classes and HA Coverage
SAP HANA high-availability mechanisms address specific failure classes:

  • Host failures
    handled by Host Auto-Failover and cluster-managed restarts
  • Primary system failures
    handled by system replication and takeover
  • Service-level failures
    handled by internal SAP HANA restart logic and cluster coordination

Coverage depends on:

  • architecture
  • topology (scale-up vs. scale-out)
  • configuration

No single mechanism covers all failure types.

7. High Availability vs. Recovery and Disaster Recovery
From a technical standpoint:

  • High availability minimizes downtime
  • Backup and recovery restore data to a previous state
  • Disaster recovery handles site-level failure domains

Although system replication may appear in both HA and DR designs, objectives, assumptions, and validation criteria differ.

High availability focuses on:

  • continuity
  • fast takeover
  • minimal disruption

Recovery and DR focus on:

  • reconstruction
  • historical restore points
  • controlled recovery after broader failures

8. Design Boundaries and Architectural Implications
From an architecture perspective:

  • high availability is achieved through combination, not a single feature
  • each HA mechanism has explicit limits
  • realistic availability expectations require alignment between:
    • failure scenarios
    • documented mechanism scope
    • operational readiness

Understanding these boundaries is essential for technically sound SAP HANA availability design.

Summary
SAP HANA provides a comprehensive, well-documented set of high-availability mechanisms.

High availability is not defined by a single feature, but by the interaction of multiple documented components, each addressing specific failure scenarios within defined technical boundaries.

By understanding the scope, mechanisms, and limits of Host Auto-Failover, System Replication, and cluster-based coordination, SAP HANA landscapes can be designed with clear, realistic availability expectations.

entry.title: 🤔 Is This the Right Way to Build Talent Intelligent Hub Integration with SAP Gene AI Hub-Extension
entry.content:
****Please check design at the bottom of this article (available for download)***

I've been sketching out an architecture for extending SAP SuccessFactors with AI-powered skills matching and requisition management. But before going down this path, I want to challenge my own assumptions. Is this the best approach, or am I overengineering?

THE PROPOSED ARCHITECTURE:

Here's what I'm considering:

🏗️ Multi-Cloud Foundation (SAP BTP)

- Cloud Foundry runtime for microservices

- HANA DB for data persistence

- Integration Suite for SuccessFactors connectivity

- Identity services for authentication

🤖AI & Analytics Layer

- SAP Generative AI Hub (GPT-4, Claude, Llama)

- Document intelligence and entity extraction

- Vector embeddings for semantic search

- Resume parsing and skills extraction

⚙️Microservices Architecture

- Requisition Analysis Service

- Skills Management Service

- Matching Service

- Custom build services for each capability

📊Advanced Data Layer

- Vector engine for embeddings

- Object store for documents

- HANA DB for structured data

- Build Process Automation for workflows

THE CRITICAL QUESTIONS I'M WRESTLING WITH:

1️⃣DO WE REALLY NEED MULTI-CLOUD COMPLEXITY?

The Good:

✅Flexibility to choose best-of-breed services

✅Avoid vendor lock-in

✅Leverage SAP BTP's enterprise capabilities

The Concerns:

⚠️Operational complexity across multiple clouds

⚠️Higher costs for integration and management

⚠️Steeper learning curve for teams

⚠️More points of failure

Alternative: Could we start with pure SuccessFactors extensions and only move to BTP if we hit limitations?

2️⃣IS MICROSERVICES THE RIGHT PATTERN HERE?

The Good:

✅Independent scaling of services

✅Team autonomy and faster deployment

✅Technology flexibility per service

The Concerns:

⚠️Distributed system complexity

⚠️Network latency between services

⚠️Harder to debug and monitor

⚠️Overkill for a small team or MVP?

Alternative: What if we started with a modular monolith and decomposed later based on actual bottlenecks?

3️⃣ARE WE READY FOR GENERATIVE AI IN PRODUCTION HR?

The Good:

✅Intelligent resume parsing and skills extraction

✅Natural language requisition analysis

✅Semantic matching beyond keyword search

✅Document generation and summarization

The Concerns:

⚠️Hallucination risks in HR decisions

⚠️Bias and fairness implications

⚠️Explainability requirements for hiring

⚠️Cost per API call at scale

⚠️Data privacy and compliance (GDPR, EEOC)

Alternative: Should we start with rule-based matching and traditional NLP, then layer in GenAI for non-critical features?

4️⃣DO WE NEED VECTOR EMBEDDINGS AND SEMANTIC SEARCH?

The Good:

✅Find similar skills even with different terminology

✅Semantic matching beyond exact keywords

✅Better candidate-requisition alignment

The Concerns:

⚠️Added infrastructure complexity (vector DB)

⚠️Embedding model maintenance and updates

⚠️Explainability: "Why was this candidate matched?"

⚠️Cost of generating and storing embeddings

Alternative: Could traditional search with synonyms and taxonomies get us 80% of the value with 20% of the complexity?

5️⃣IS THE INTEGRATION LAYER OVER-ARCHITECTED?

The Good:

✅Clean separation of concerns

✅Reusable integration patterns

✅API management and event mesh

The Concerns:

⚠️Do we need event mesh for this use case?

⚠️Is Build Process Automation necessary or could we use simpler workflows?

⚠️Are we adding layers that slow down development?

Alternative: Direct SuccessFactors OData APIs with simple retry logic?

WHAT MIGHT BE A SIMPLER STARTING POINT?

Phase 1: Validate the Value

- Build a SuccessFactors extension app (UI5)

- Use SuccessFactors APIs directly

- Simple keyword-based matching

- Manual review and approval workflows

- Prove the business value first

Phase 2: Add Intelligence Gradually

- Introduce resume parsing (traditional NLP)

- Add skills taxonomy and synonyms

- Basic analytics dashboard

- Measure improvement over baseline

Phase 3: Scale What Works

- Only then consider microservices if we have clear scaling needs

- Add GenAI for specific high-value use cases

- Introduce vector search if keyword matching proves insufficient

- Expand to multi-cloud only if we need capabilities not in SuccessFactors

THE REAL QUESTIONS:

💭Am I solving for scale we don't have yet?

💭Am I choosing technology because it's interesting vs. because it's necessary?

💭What's the simplest thing that could possibly work?

💭How do we balance innovation with pragmatism?

💭What would a true MVP look like?

WHAT I'D LOVE YOUR INPUT ON:

🔹Have you built similar HR/talent extensions? What worked? What didn't?

🔹Where have you seen GenAI add real value in recruiting vs. just hype?

🔹When is microservices worth it vs. premature optimization?

🔹How do you balance "enterprise-grade" with "ship fast and learn"?

🔹What would you do differently?

I'm genuinely torn between building something robust and future-proof vs. starting simple and evolving based on real needs.

What's your take? Is this architecture the right approach, or should we start simpler and earn our way to complexity?

Drop your thoughts below - especially if you think I'm overcomplicating this! 👇

#EnterpriseArchitecture #SAPSuccessFactors #SAPBTP #TalentManagement #AIinHR #Microservices #CloudArchitecture #SoftwareEngineering #TechLeadership #ArchitecturalDecisions #BuildOrBuy #MVPFirst #OverengineeringDebate

Hi @jay_san_nz ,

See you at CodeJam 🙂

entry.title: Re: characteristic values getting assigned automatically...
entry.content:
Hello @KVKUMAR ,

Can you please check the Configuration Profile in Tcode- CU42. This can be a reason. Check the settings for your Material’s configuration profile. In the user interface tab -> Check if the "Screen depiction" is set to "No screen" or if "Skip initial screen" is checked. If these are active, SAP will only show the screen if there is an error or an incomplete mandatory characteristic.

Vaibhav

entry.title: Re: Capability to Replicate Composite Custom MDF objects...
entry.content: How do you think to cater this ?

entry.title: SPAU cannot change status Partly obsolete, completely implemented, has to be confirmed
entry.content:
Dear Colleagues,

I have next situations:

During  the upgrade QAS system we used SPDD/SPAU requests from DEV system.

As result, after upgrade we can see minimum delta in SPAU (between DEV-QA), but one of SAP note is cannot be confirmed/adjusted. It has status "Partly obsolete, completely implemented, has to be confirmed".

When I choose option "Confirm partly obsolete SAP Note"  and press Execute then  message "Action successfully completed" appears, but status of sapnote does not change (still partly obsolete): 

I checked via SNOTE, it looks OK, sapnote "Completely implemented", new version downloaded.

I don't know what to do, should I ignore it?

entry.title: SAP S/4 HANA OData v4 APIs available - Quick Reference and available in SAP API Business Hub
entry.content:
SAP S/4HANA exposes a rich catalog of OData V4 APIs on the SAP API Business Hub, enabling modern, lightweight, and efficient integration. These APIs support RESTful access with improved performance, reduced payload sizes (JSON), and advanced metadata modeling. You can explore and test all available OData V4 APIs—both for S/4HANA On‑Premise —directly from the SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

Administration Notes

SAP provides two key transactions for OData V4 lifecycle:

  • /IWBEP/V4_ADMIN → Service registration in backend
  • /IWFND/V4_ADMIN → Service group publishing on Gateway hub
These ensure proper activation before consumption in tools like SAPUI5, SAP Build Apps, SAP Build Process Automation, SAP Integration Suite,  Postman, or custom integrations.

Where to Find OData V4 APIs (363 Objects - as of 15th Feb 2026)

All S/4HANA OData V4 services are available on:

ODATA V4 API : https://api.sap.com/package/S4HANAOPAPI/odatav4

entry.title: Re: Deep Dive into SAP Datasphere Object Store of BDC: Benefits, Architecture and implementation
entry.content:
Great blog post @SreekanthSurampally - think it's quite important to have articles on the Object Store architecture, given its novelty in the overall Datasphere/BDC.

Think that there is still quite a lot confusion related to the Object Store vs. more 'older' HANA Data Lake (relational one), and which one should be used, for what exactly, how much they cost compared to each other and compared to the traditional HANA Cloud 'disk' storage etc.

Yet, what I still cannot fully comprehend - Object Store (HANA Data Lake on Files) costs roughly ~0.075 CU per GB-Month, while traditional HANA Cloud disk costs roughly ~0.18 CU per GB-Month (OK, the former requires also some compute and requests in addition, while the latter requires memory/compute). It's (very roughly, if we also disregard compression etc.) x2.4 cost rationing, and not say x10 rationing (that one might expect), for a non-negligible performance and modelling overhead.

To put things into perspective, in the same manner, the 'older' (relational engine) HANA Data Lake storage costs roughly ~0.56 CU per GB-Month (OK, compute included), which then makes it even more expensive than the HANA Cloud disk storage - cannot see then why would it be even ever used if more expensive and slower and more complex(?!)

Or maybe I'm missing some important points in reading the whole thing? 🙂

entry.content: @iperez-sofos What you need is a freestyle app that scans a barcode and navigates to the List Report app, which then opens the Object Page. An alternative approach is to create the entire app based on the Custom Page template. You can create the first screen in any format, and with some controller logic, navigate to the Object Page.

Hi,

I've got a strage case in a S4/HANA 2023 FPS02 box.

All custom reports (Z*), and only created in IT language, when are used in other languages then IT  have wrong menu transtalation and column ALV translation with output.

As example, here e custom report created in IT, it's ok in IT language, but when I run it in EN...

In custom reports created in EN, and run in IT language, are good.

 

Already try a full system reboot.

Already try with GUI 7.80, 7.70 and 8.00... webgui of course.

Already try note 413131 - SEC_SUPPORT: Revision of texts in the role menu

Already try note 538328 - Testi mancanti nel menu utente

Have you got any idea?

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Deep Dive into SAP Datasphere Object Store of BDC: Benefits, Architecture and implementation
entry.content:
Really appreciate the depth and clarity in this write-up. The way you explained the Object Store architecture within SAP Datasphere and its positioning inside SAP Business Data Cloud makes it much easier to understand where it fits in real project scenarios.

 

The breakdown of benefits — especially around cost optimization, scalability with Spark processing, and medallion-style layering — was very practical. This is particularly relevant for customers handling large historical datasets or data product scenarios. Now 1 Question.

1. In hybrid architectures where some datasets remain in in-memory HANA tables for performance, how do you typically decide what should stay in the Object Store vs. what should be modeled in core Datasphere tables? Are there practical sizing or latency benchmarks you recommend?

entry.title: India : Practical use inquiry on the "Invoice Reduction" function
entry.content:
Dear Experts,

Do you use the “Invoice Reduction” function in Create Supplier Invoice / MIRO in India?

I have also consulted Joule for Consultants; however, I am not in a position to confirm whether the responses fully reflect actual business practice. I would greatly appreciate your insights based on real project or customer experience in India, or a Joule-based answer that has been validated in practice.

Our intention is to understand the actual practice in India.

The background of this question relates to the “Invoice Reduction” function available during supplier invoice creation in SAP. We would like to understand whether this function is commonly used in India and whether it is typically applicable for customers there.

For reference, in Japan this function is generally not used. When there is a price difference at the time of invoicing, it is typically handled by either:

  • correcting the PO price, or using subsequent debit/credit functionality,

rather than directly reducing the invoice amount via the invoice reduction function.

Could you kindly share the common practice in India based on your experience?

Thank you very much in advance for your guidance.

entry.title: Re: Unable to add email domain
entry.content: As per KBA 3025172 - How to add/remove email domains - SAP for Me, only Super Administrators and Cloud Administrators can use the Maintain Allowed Company Domains application to register and maintain a list of official Internet domains for their company. Since you are neither a super admin nor a cloud admin, this application  Maintain Allowed Company Domains is not accessible for you. For data security reason, our SAP always recommend customer to manage the domain by themselves. Thus please contact below super admin or cloud admin to help add the domain.

entry.title: El reto real del CFO actual
entry.content:
La mayoría de las organizaciones aún operan con procesos financieros que:

  • Dependen de reconciliaciones manuales
  • Tienen información fragmentada entre FI, CO y módulos satélite
  • Cierran tarde y con alta presión operativa
  • Reaccionan, en lugar de anticipar

El problema no es el equipo. Es la arquitectura financiera heredada.

SAP S/4HANA: Finanzas en tiempo real, no al cierre del mes

SAP S/4HANA redefine el rol de Finanzas a partir de un concepto clave: el Universal Journal.

Esto permite:

  • Un solo registro financiero (ACDOCA)
  • Eliminación de reconciliaciones entre FI y CO
  • Información consistente desde el origen
  • Reportes en tiempo real, sin esperar al cierre

Para el CFO, esto se traduce en algo muy concreto: confianza en los números, en cualquier momento.

Cierre financiero más rápido, con menos esfuerzo

Uno de los mayores dolores del CFO es el cierre mensual.

Con SAP S/4HANA:

  • Se automatizan tareas repetitivas
  • Se reducen ajustes de último minuto
  • El cierre deja de ser un evento traumático

El beneficio no es solo cerrar más rápido, sino liberar tiempo del equipo financiero para análisis y estrategia.

De control operativo a visión estratégica

SAP S/4HANA permite a Finanzas:

  • Analizar rentabilidad en tiempo real
  • Monitorear flujo de efectivo de forma predictiva
  • Detectar desviaciones antes de que se conviertan en problemas
  • Integrar analítica directamente en los procesos (Embedded Analytics)

Esto cambia la conversación del CFO con la Dirección General: de explicar resultados a guiar decisiones.

Automatización e inteligencia: menos operación, más valor

S/4HANA, combinado con soluciones como SAP BTP, IA y herramientas de automatización, habilita:

  • Conciliaciones automáticas
  • Cash Application inteligente
  • Detección de anomalías
  • Forecast financiero más preciso

No se trata de reemplazar personas, sino de elevar el rol del talento financiero.

Cloud, control y escalabilidad

Para muchos CFOs, la pregunta no es si migrar, sino cómo hacerlo sin perder control.

SAP S/4HANA ofrece:

  • Modelos Cloud y On-Premise
  • Seguridad y cumplimiento normativo
  • Escalabilidad para crecer sin rediseñar Finanzas cada pocos años

La clave es elegir el camino correcto alineado al modelo de negocio.

 

#CFO #SAP #S4HANA #FinanzasEstratégicas #TransformacionFinanciera #ERP #AI
